channel.title: Blog posts on eddiequinn.xyz
channel.description: Recent content in Blog posts on eddiequinn.xyz

item.title: Crypto-watch: Rune
item.description: Introduction As time goes on, the crypto market is beginning to mimic the legacy financial system; this is especially true in the notion of lack of privacy. Everywhere you go, you need to provide KYC (know your customer), all the services you use require an intermediary, and everything you do is tracked to a T. This was not Satoshi’s vision when he gifted us the block-chain, and it is concerning to many of us.
item.content:
Introduction

As time goes on, the crypto market is beginning to mimic the legacy financial system; this is especially true in the notion of lack of privacy. Everywhere you go, you need to provide KYC (know your customer), all the services you use require an intermediary, and everything you do is tracked to a T. This was not Satoshi’s vision when he gifted us the block-chain, and it is concerning to many of us.

This is not to say that no crypto projects are working to keep this industry free from big brother, the two big ones being ThorChain (RUNE) & Monero (XMR). Projects like these have a real potential to change the financial system forever

I know I promised an article on how blockchain works in my Proton Mail - A wolf in sheep’s clothing entry. I am working on that right now, but it is a far larger topic than I realised, and for me to give it the attention it deserves, I want to spend a few weeks researching it first. However, I did not want to leave you hanging, so to satisfy your blockchain knowledge wants, here is a deep dive on RUNE (my personal favourite crypto as of yet).

Before we begin however I want to get something out of the way. I am NOT a financial adviser, please do not take financial advice from a tiny little blog on the internet. This article is for education purposes only. While I do personally hold RUNE, I am in NO WAY advising you to do so.

History & background

ThorChain was founded by a group of anonymous coders, at a Binance hackathon in 2018. The project technically has no founder and none of the 18 self-organized devs of the project has a formal title of any kind (you likely see how this appeals to me). Most are still anon, but a few have been revealed, namely Chad Barraford.

After raising $2m initial capital through private sales and seed sales in 2018 and 2019, and raising an additional $250,000 from its IEO on Binance. ThorChain’s first iteration (chaos net) went live in the summer of 2020. This was followed by the fully functional multi-chain chaos net in early 2020. It went on to become the back-end for the DEX BEP-Swap in September 2020

Up until this point, almost all the resources associated with the project (e.g. the website) have been developed by the ThorChain community. This would be concerning if it wasn’t for the absolute transparency of ThorChain’s core operations. Development updates are posted every week, and treasury reports every month, all of its code is open source. As of the time of writing has been audited 10 times by reputable names such as ‘Certic’ and ‘The Gauntlet’

What is it meant to be

Rune is meant to be a DEX protocol that allows for multiple cryptocurrencies to be exchanged across different blockchains. Unlike other solutions for this problem, ThorChain allows for this to be done natively and done w/out wrapping.

The other main contender in this space is UniSwap, which requires the factors discussed above. As well as this only lets you exchange cryptocurrencies on the same block-chain, and any across chain swaps require Wrapped assets

How it works

ThorChain works differently from this because it is an automated market. It uses a ratio of two cryptos inside a pool to determine the price. Cryptos are pooled with RUNE, the native coin of ThorChain. This is done with the intent to minimise the number of pools, which in turn

  • Maximises liquidity
  • Provides economic incentives to make ThorChain work

Thor was built using the cosmos SDK. Uses proof of stake consensus algorithm based on Tendermint. Currently, the network is run by 70 ± validator nodes which coordinate cross-chain transactions, but theoretically, it can support up to 360. ThorChain is intended to act as the BackEnd of a DEX interface, currently, there are multiple front-ends to ThorChain (mostly made by the ThorChain community)

Nodes Vaults
ThorChain nodes are responsible for witnessing transactions that occur in other block-chains. This process is mentioned below in the section labelled “the swapping process”. They also send and receive crypto from various wallets that they all have collective custody. Sending any crypto out of these collectively custodied wallets, however, requires the consensus of 2/3 of all active validator nodes ThorChain vaults store all the cryptocurrencies offered by the ThorChain protocol. This crypto comes from liquidity providers who pool an equivalent amount of Rune/Btc Rune/Eth or any other crypto offered by ThorChain.
ThorChain nodes must bond, or stake, 300,000 RUNE (£2.4m at the time of writing) to accept. In addition to this nodes must stake an amount of RUNE 1.4x the value of the cryptocurrency stored in its vaults. This is done so that if they attempt to steal the money in those vaults, the money they lose is greater than the money they gain via the process of slashing. On top of this ThorChain nodes are expected to attempt to stay anonymous; A large part of why delegating Rune is not allowed It should also be mentioned that ThorChain does allow asymmetrical deposits also. An asymmetrical deposit is where a user will despot unequal values of two assets in the pool. In their own words “a user deposits $2000 of BTC and $0 of RUNE into a BTC/RUNE pool. Under the hood, the member is given ownership of the pool that takes into account the slip created in price. The liquidity provider will end up with <$1000 in BTC and <$1000 in RUNE. The deeper the pool the closer to a total of $2000 the member will own
ThorChain Validator nodes are churned every three days for two reasons. To ehance the security of the protocol, and bring about any protocol upgrades ThorChain decides which cryptos to support using a pending pool. Investors can pool Rune and the crypto they want to see supported. The pool with the most assets is then added to ThorChain by nodes, making it trade-able by users

ThorChain validator nodes and liquidity providers earn a large cut of block-chain rewards and protocol fees in exchange for their service. To ensure the ThorChain remains secure, a mechanism called the incentive pendulum will increase/decrease rewards to nodes/liquidity providers to increase/decrease their staked rune

The swapping process & AMM mechanism

As mentioned before, all swaps facilitated with ThorChain trade against RUNE. The reason for this is that it is inefficient to create a pool for every possible cryptocurrency pair. To quote the ThorChain website “If ThorChain supports 1,000 chains, it would only need 1,000 pools. A competitor would need a whopping 499,500 pools”. This large number of pools dilutes liquidity, taking away from the trading experience.

Because all coins trade against RUNE, liquidity providers need to provide equal values of BTC and RUNE. As with other AMM style DEX protocols, arbitrage trades are incentivised to ensure this is the case. This is because changes would cause a discrepancy, and the arbitrage traders would jump on this. This would cause the price to go back to what it should be, meaning the process is self-regulating. This reliance on arbitrage traders is what allows ThorChain to remain decoupled from price oracles like a ChainLink.

Natively the reliance on trading against RUNE causes the issue of what if someone doesn’t want to trade against RUNE. This issue is however solved by front end DEX interfaces, which automate this process. In the background, the following is occurring (assuming a BTC/ETH trade):

  1. BTC is being sent to the BTC vault custodied by ThorChain validators
  2. The ThorChain validators pickup the transaction on the BTC blockchain
  3. The price of the BTC is determined by its pairing against RUNE, the same goes for ETH
  4. The nodes sent the appropriate amount of ETH to your stated ETH address

This whole swap process can take as little as 10 seconds, depending on the coins being sent and the whole process is paid for in RUNE. This rune then goes to paying network fees in the corresponding coins. In addition to this, it also goes toward a slip fee which is based on how much you’re changing the price, calculated using the ratio remaining in the pool. This slip fee is put in place to prevent whales

The tokenomics of Rune

The tokenomics of RUNE Rune began as a BEP2 token in 2019, with an initial max supply of 1bn. This was reduced to 500m at the end of 2019. Rune now exists natively on the ThorChain blockchain, but there is still plenty of Rune in circulation on BEP2 (Binance) and ERC20 (Ethereum).

Of runes total supply:

  • 30m sold to seed investors
  • 70m sold to pre-sale investors
  • 20m in an IDO, although 17m was subsequently burned
  • 105 million are held by the team to fund operations
  • The remaining 205m have been allocated for block rewards

The pre-mined block rewards for validators and liquidity providers have a ten-year vesting schedule.

Were it not for the generous team and private sale allocations, Rune would likely have the best tokenomics of any other crypto on the market. At any given time ThorChain validators must be staking rune worth 2x the total value locked by liquidity providers. The fact that DEX users need Rune to trade on ThorChain front ends, means that it has a similar economic profile to Eth (used to pay gas on Etherium)

It is likely the demand for ThorCHhain will continue to grow as it adds support for more blockchains and grows its ecosystem

  • ThorChain - pBFT chain optimised for lighting fast layer 1 digital asset swaps
  • Aesir Protocol - Forkless governance protocol for ThorChain ecosystem
  • Yggdrasil Protocol - Highly scaleable sharing solution for ThorChain
  • Asgardex - Fast and secure liquidity interface for ThorChain
  • Flash Network - Layer2 network for cross-network instance asset exchange
  • Bifrost Protocol - Cross-chain bridge protocol for ThorChain

Large amounts of Rune will also be required to bootstrap new assets to ThorChain. This is since nodes automatically begin accepting assets with the largest amounts of Rune pooled against them. In addition to this, ThorChain is also aiming to create

  • A decentralised stablecoin
  • A series of cross-chain DEFI protocols

item.title: Protonmail, a wolf in sheep's clothing?
item.description: “I’m not upset that you lied to me, I’m upset that from now on I can’t believe you” ~ Friedrich Nietzsche This article is going to discuss the controversial issue of why does Protonmail not actually measure up to what it claims to be. This article is going to annoy a lot of people inside the alt-tech domain, and I’m okay with this. This blog was never meant to be a place where I state popular opinions on things, it was always going to be a verbose and unapologetic collection of articles on subjects I find interesting.
item.content:
“I’m not upset that you lied to me, I’m upset that from now on I can’t believe you” ~ Friedrich Nietzsche

This article is going to discuss the controversial issue of why does Protonmail not actually measure up to what it claims to be. This article is going to annoy a lot of people inside the alt-tech domain, and I’m okay with this. This blog was never meant to be a place where I state popular opinions on things, it was always going to be a verbose and unapologetic collection of articles on subjects I find interesting. I am a customer of Protonmail and have been for many years, and I am likely to remain so for many years to come, but this does not mean that Protonmail does not deserve to be critiqued.

##What is Protonmail

Before we begin I’m going to explain briefly what Protonmail is, because while this name is thrown around a lot inside the alt-tech industry, few people outside of this group know what it is. Protonmail is an end-to-end encrypted email service. It was founded in 2013 in Geneva, Switzerland. It uses client-side encryption to protect both email content, as well as user data before being sent to the centralised Protonmail server, a service not offered by the leading entities within the email space i.e. Gmail or Outlook. The service can be accessed through the web-mail client, the TOR network, and dedicated iOS and Android apps.

Encryption wise Protonmail uses public-key cryptography. When the user creates a Protonmail account, their browser generates a pair of public and private RSA keys. The public key is used to encrypt the user’s email and other user data, while the private key is capable of decrypting the user data, and is symmetrically encrypted with the user’s mailbox password. This symmetrical encryption happens in the user’s web browser using the popular AES-256 algorithm. Upon logging in, the user has to provide their password to decrypt the mailbox. The decryption takes place client-side, either inside the web browser or natively inside the app. The public key and the encrypted private key are both stored on Protonmail servers, thus Protonmail staff are unable to retrieve user emails or reset user mailbox passwords. This system absolves Protonmail from the ability to:

  • Store unencrypted data or mailbox passwords
  • Divulge the contents of past emails
  • Decrypt the mailbox if requested by a court order

Protonmail exclusively supports HTTPS and uses TLS with ephemeral key exchange to encrypt all internet traffic between users and Protonmail servers. Their 4096-bit RSA SSL certificate is signed by QuoVadis Trustlink Schweiz AG and supports Extended Validation, Certificate Transparency, Public Key Pinning, and Strict Transport Security. Protonmail holds an A+ rating from Qualys SSL Labs.

If all of that went over your head it means that on paper, Protonmail is a safe and secure way to store and send an email.

##The problem

Now all of the claims made by Protonmail are quite bold, especially the ones about ‘end-to-end encryption and the ‘anonymous email service’. In the past, there was a private email service called cock.li (yes this was a real thing), it was marketed as a private email client, but in their words, “with cocks”. If you signed up you would get an email with a cock themed email domain, for example:

  • cock.li
  • 420blaze.it
  • waifu.club
  • cocaine.ninja
  • horsefucker.org

Now you may be questioning what this has to do with Protonmail, and I wouldn’t blame you for that. Cock.li notoriously had a section at the bottom right of their site titled “how can I trust you”, to which they responded with “you can’t”. What they said was that they didn’t parse your email to provide you with targeted ads, nor does it read your email unless court ordered. However, they were very open about the fact that it is 100% possible for them to read the user’s email. The reason for this is that IMAP/SMTP does not provide user/client-side encryption. Any encryption implementation would also be useless because while email can be stored as encrypted, emails could easily be intercepted by sniffing

  • the SMTP port whilst being sent/received
  • the IMAP port whilst the client is reading them

They stated the way the user could achieve privacy with email would be to encrypt their emails using PGP via a mail client ad-don like Enigmail, or downloading their mail locally with POP and regularly deleting mail from their centralised server.

Now while you may rip cock.li for their weird domain names, they laid out in honest terms what a private email service can do for you, which is not much. Nothing about email is private because it was never designed to be private. You can layer privacy on top of it, but fundamentally no matter how well a house is built, if the foundations are weak then it will eventually collapse. Regardless of the claims of cock.li or Protonmail, on a technical level, you still have to put your trust in the claims of the service provider not to invade your privacy.

All this being said, it is perfectly understandable from a capitalist perspective why Protonmail would not take this 100% transparency approach. Protonmail is trying to build their market presence, and if they were to come out and say openly that they can only do as much as outlook or Gmail in the way of guaranteeing privacy, they likely wouldn’t have found the success they have.

##The claims

Now with this information being established, the claims made by Protonmail begin to fall apart. When looking specifically at the claim about end-to-end encryption, in the past a security researcher called ‘Nadmin Kobeissi’ and a YouTuber called ‘Live Overflow’ stated that their encryption is not nearly as robust as they claim. The argument they presented was that Protonmail should discontinue their web-mail client due to JavaScript’s shortcomings with the implementation of PGP encryption inside a web-mail ecosystem. Nadmin’s core argument is that “Protonmail’s cryptographic architecture ultimately does not guarantee end-to-end encryption for the majority of users”, the majority of users referring to the users who access their mailbox via their browser as opposed to natively via an app.

The reason for this discrepancy between the web-mail app and the smartphone app is technical but simple to communicate; its about trust. Protonmail provides a user-friendly interface to facilitate the exchange of encryption keys using PGP protocol, currently considered one of the highest standards for securing communications. The problem with PGP is that its implementation used to be too complex for the end-user. However after the NSA leaks this lit a fire underneath the alt-tech communities proverbial arse to make this technology more user friendly. This fire leads to the inception of Signal messenger, and our very own Protonmail. The key difference between Signal messenger and Protonmail however, is that Signal messenger does not provide a web-based interface, only the native mobile and desktop apps. Protonmail being an email client did not have the luxury to circumvent the web-mail ecosystem at first, and only later brought about the ability the view/interact with your mailbox with an app. Now the problem with this is that every time you log into your Promotional account inside your web browser, you have to completely trust the JavaScript being run is correctly implementing PGP and is not trying to steal your private keys and read your emails. This problem is minimised with the apps, because for the app to be available the code needs to be both signed by both the author and the platform, in this case, the author being Protonmail, and the platform being either Google or Apple. This discrepancy is what causes web-mail services to be verifiably less secure than native apps. However, it is important to mention that Protonmail does not hide this, and even states on their threat model page that Protonmail is not for the next Edward Snowden, it is for average people who just want to break out advertisement industrial complex.

Protonmail’s system does however also fall short because of the reasons discussed above the shortcoming of email itself as a protocol. There is a difference between inter-domain email and intra-domain email in this use case. Intra-domain would be the case where one Protonmail user is sending an email to another Protonmail user. In this case, it can be encrypted provided the Protonmail offers this. However in the case where a user was to send an email to a Gmail address, this would be an inter-domain transfer, and iwould be not possible for the email to remain encrypted. This transfer requires two different mail transfer agents, in this case using SMTP port 25 which cannot be encrypted. This would have to occur in plain text, and anyone sniffing the traffic on port 25 would be able to see all traffic coming in and out in plain text. As well as this, regardless of if it is inter or intra domain, the meta-data in your emails in both of these cases would not be encrypted. This metadata includes your IP address, the email server’s IP address, the name of your computer, time stamps, subjects, and the email address of both the sender and the recipient.

With all this established, we will re-examine the claims made by Protonmail about end-to-end encryption where they themselves say “we use end-to-end encryption and zero access encryption to secure your email. This means even we cannot decrypt and read your emails. As a result, your encrypted emails cannot be shared with third parties.” Now the only way to read this as factually correct is that they cannot read your emails when you are sending emails inter-domain, because any other domain would be in plain text. Taking this aside, while you can encrypt the body of an email using PGP using proton mail, the metadata is still plain text, and this metadata can sometimes provide far more useful information than the body could ever hope to. The reason for this is metadata is far less subjective, and therefore far easier to automatically prepare, process, and analyse.

Then of course there is the onion site, which many agree is useless. The reason for this is because it has a fatal flaw that will deanonymise you. Despite the site having a V3 address, as soon as you click ‘Create Account’ you are instantly brought to a clear web address. I try to abide by the mantra to not assign blame to malice where it could just as easily be explained by incompetence, but this flaw in their system more or less makes the use of an onion site pointless. This is further compacted by the fact that you either have to provide a recovery email/phone number to create this account or pay using a debit/credit card. In other words, there is no way to create an anonymous account on Protonmail, which negates their claim of anonymous email.

There claim states “No personal information is required to create your secure email account. By default, we do not keep any logs which can be liked to your anonymous email account. Your privacy comes first”. The first half of this claim is an outright lie, the only way around this would be to use a burner phone with a prepaid sim, but that is hardly a solution for your average user which is Protonmail’s target market. The other half about IP’s is nice to say, but in reality, it breaks down. They may not keep IP logs, but in practice, they require your IP for the service to work because the networking protocol used by Protonmail. For this second half of the claim to be true requires you the user to trust them not to log it.

##This is not necessarily their fault

Now following this article and the points raised may cause you to ask “well if Protonmail can’t be trusted, what email provider can be?” Unfortunate the bad news here is that none of them can be. I do plan to one day do an article on the history of email, but as I said earlier email is not secure because it was never designed to be secure. Even in the case where you was to self-host your email server and set it up in such a way that the data would be lost if the intrusion was detected:

  • the SMTP port will always have the flaw that it cannot be encrypted
  • Intra domain messages will always be sent in plain text

Email is the legacy system that cannot be replaced, and the moral of the story here is that your threat model has zero-tolerance for data leaks then email is not something you should be using. All this being said, Protonmail is still a far better option than Gmail or Outlook, and that is why despite all the points I’ve raised in this article I still use it. Sometimes it is simply about taking the least worst option, and in time hopefully, some of the issues with Protonmail that are their fault will be plugged.

item.title: What are NFT-Domains and why should you care?
item.description: “NFTs are the future. But it can be scary to get started.” NFTs have been all the rage recently within the crypto space, and while they do deserve their own article, for now, I’m going to assume you know the basics. That being that now files can be committed to the blockchain, making it nigh-on impossible to get rid of them, these files can be image files, text files, and now domain names.
item.content:
“NFTs are the future. But it can be scary to get started.”

NFTs have been all the rage recently within the crypto space, and while they do deserve their own article, for now, I’m going to assume you know the basics. That being that now files can be committed to the blockchain, making it nigh-on impossible to get rid of them, these files can be image files, text files, and now domain names.

With NFT domain names, it opens up the advent of people actually being able to own their domain names. Now before you send me an angry email saying “but I own my domain name, I have a permanent lease on it” I think it’s important to understand that traditional domain names cannot really be owned. It is impossible to own a traditional domain name, everyone (including the MAANG overlords) leases these domain names from the registry. You may pay a yearly fee for the right to use that domain name, but that is a far cry for outright ownership. NFT domains open up the advent for you to pay once, and permanently own your NFT domain, for example, I own the domain name ‘eddies-wallet.x’ (feel free to send some crypto to buy me a coffee some time). To make this possible, NFT domains are a product of a new web standard commonly referred to as Web3, an up and coming standard to enable a decentralized web (similar to NFT’s by themselves this 100% deserved its own article, so standby for that).

With traditional domain .com era domain names, the regulatory body that umbrellas these is ICANN. ICANN is a complicated beast to tackle in one statement but to keep this simple let’s just say they are the gatekeeper of the internet. They’re a non-profit that manages the domain name system to make sure they are all accessible, and when you type in eddiequinn.xyz it’s that site that loads and not another. This colossal system many of us take for granted when surfing the web is far more complex than many of us realise, and ICANN is constantly updating and managing to make sure this centralized system runs smoothly.

This system, while convenient, still has a major glaring problem. When you lease something the powers that be could one day just say “no you don’t get the right to use that anymore”. In a centralised system, someone always has the keys to your kingdom and it is seldom you. In this environment the benefits of blockchain shine, allowing for decentralisation inside this centralised ecosystem. Now as is always, blockchain deserves its own article but for now, we’ll just quickly address it. A blockchain is essentially a chain of records, stored on thousands of different machines across the world. The reason blockchain has been associated with crypto is because in its purest form it is essentially a ledger of transactions, open for anyone to see, which is stored across thousands of nodes, all constantly verifying both the database and each other. No one person is holding the keys, and this distributed ownership makes the scenario of losing your kingdom almost impossible to come into fruition. Income’s NFT domains, buy it once, own it forever. It piggybacks off this newly created blockchain eco-system, and now the keys to your kingdom are held by no one but you. No one can just remove your presence from the internet.

However, this new system has its own problems, as I mentioned before the centralised system is convenient. First off, you cannot buy a .com or .net domain name from this system, all the legacy domain names you’re used to are from that old centralized system, and the powers that be have a vested interest in keeping this subscription-based model in place. Web3 domains have to be unique, therefore, and thus far they are all pretty goofy

  • .zil
  • .crypto
  • .x

All of these names in their current state are like this for a reason however, they aim to act somewhat as a username for crypto-wallets. This system is what allows you, my dear reader, to buy me a coffee when you’re done reading this article, simply by typing eddies-wallet.x as the address. You no longer have to type in a massive random string. This system actually echos why domain names were originally made, because fundamentally every website is stored on a server, and it is far easier to remember google.com rather than the IP address of that server. This current use case, however, isn’t what these domains are limited to, 10 years from now when Web3 hopefully will be the standard system used, you will be able to host websites on them.

It is also worth mentioning that the two biggest blockchains holding these NFTs are Ethereum and Zilliqa. Out of the current ten domain extensions being sold via this method, 90% of them are on Ethereum, with the only one being held on the Zilliqa blockchain being ‘.zil’. This is not necessarily something unexpected, however, given that Ethereum is the second-largest blockchain with the largest being bitcoin.

Theoretically, you can use your NFT domain for something like a WordPress website, but not to the extent that would be ideal. The problem is right now NFT domain names are accessible out of the box mainstream browsers, right now only tech-head websites like Opera and Brave actually support them. You can load Web3 domains in google chrome, but it requires more modification than would be ideal. This means that the average user won’t be able to just pull out their iPhone, and type into safari eddies-wallet.x, and these sites aren’t even recognised by google.

Taking this rabbit hole server, the two building blocks for any website are the domain name and the WebHost. We now have a way to get the domain name decentralized, but this is basically useless if a ghost or AWS decided tomorrow to take you down. This is where decentralised web hosting comes in, via the ‘InterPlanetary File System’ or IPFS. IPFS works by storing pieces of your website across multiple nodes, meaning that there is no single point of failure. This system has redundancy built in to prevent any one person from being able to just remove your website files. This sounds great, but IPFS is still growing, they have quirks similar to Web3 domain names. At this moment in time, it can only really host static single page HTML sites, meaning no WordPress. Until this system picks up, the websites hosted with it will be similar to the 2000’s aesthetic.

This is by no means saying you should not invest in an NFT domain, unlike most NFT art projects they have legitimate utility. This utility is only going to grow in the future, and as this utility grows so will the price. Now as a disclaimer I AM NOT A FINANCIAL ADVISER, do not take financial advice from a random blog on the internet. I personally invested in one, and plan to invest in two more when I have some additional cash lying about, but that’s me. What I will say is if you plan on getting one of these while they are still relatively cheap, id strongly recommend using UnstopableDomains, and if you want to support this blog please use this link when you purchase you brand new domain name.
